subject: Physics
q: Two identical antennas mounted on identical towers are separated from each other by a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. What should nearly be the minimum height of receiving antenna to receive the signals in line of sight ? (Assume radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
question: Two identical antennas mounted on identical towers are separated from each other by a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>45</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. What should nearly be the minimum height of receiving antenna to receive the signals in line of sight ? (Assume radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>)
solution:
D=22Rh

h=D28R=4528×6400 km≅39.55 m

subject: Physics
q: A square wave of the modulating signal is shown in the figure. The carrier wave is given by Ct=5sin8πtVolt. The modulation index is
question: A square wave of the modulating signal is shown in the figure. The carrier wave is given by Ct=5sin8πtVolt. The modulation index is
solution:
Given here, Ct=5sin8πt volts.

From the carrier wave equation we have, AC=5 and from the graph we have, Am=1.

Modulation Index μ=AmAC=15=0.2

Where, 

Am= amplitude of modulating signal

Ac= amplitude of carrier wave

subject: Physics
q: A FM Broad cast transmitter, using modulating signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mi>kHz</mi></math> has a deviation ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math>. The Bandwidth required for transmission is :
question: A FM Broad cast transmitter, using modulating signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mi>kHz</mi></math> has a deviation ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math>. The Bandwidth required for transmission is :
solution:
Given

FM broadcast

Modulating frequency =20 kHz=f

Deviation ratio = frequency deviation  modulating frequency =∆ff

⇒ Frequency deviation, Δf=f×10

=20 kHz×10=200 kHz

⇒ Bandwidth =2f+Δf

=220+200 kHz

=440 kHz

subject: Physics
q: A radar has a power of $1 \mathrm{~Kw}$ and is operating at a frequency of $10 \mathrm{~GHz}$. It is located on a mountain top of height $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The maximum distance upto which it can detect object located on the surface of the earth (Radius of earth $=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$ ) is
question: A radar has a power of $1 \mathrm{~Kw}$ and is operating at a frequency of $10 \mathrm{~GHz}$. It is located on a mountain top of height $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The maximum distance upto which it can detect object located on the surface of the earth (Radius of earth $=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$ ) is
solution: Maximum distance on earth where object can be detected is $\mathrm{d}$, then $\begin{array}{ll} & (h+R)^2=d^2+R^2 \\ \Rightarrow & d^2=h^2+2 R h \\ \text { since } & h < < R, \quad \Rightarrow d^2=2 h R \\ \Rightarrow & d=\sqrt{2(500)\left(6.4 \times 10^6\right)}=80 \mathrm{~km} \end{array}$

subject: Physics
q: The maximum amplitude for an amplitude modulated wave is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> while the minimum amplitude is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The modulation index is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
question: The maximum amplitude for an amplitude modulated wave is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> while the minimum amplitude is found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The modulation index is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
Amax=Ac+Am=12

Amin=Ac-Am=3

⇒Ac=152 and  Am=92

Modulation index =AmAc=92152=0.6

⇒x=1

subject: Physics
q: A massage signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>  is used to modulate a carrier signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math>. The bandwidth of the amplitude modulated wave is
question: A massage signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>  is used to modulate a carrier signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math>. The bandwidth of the amplitude modulated wave is
solution:
The relation between bandwidth and the signal frequency is given by

Bandwidth=2× signal frequency   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameter into equation (1) to calculate the required bandwidth.

Bandwidth= 2 × 3 kHz= 6 kHz

subject: Physics
q: The modulation index for an A.M. wave having maximum and minimum peak to peak voltages of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></math> respectively is :
question: The modulation index for an A.M. wave having maximum and minimum peak to peak voltages of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mV</mi></math> respectively is :
solution:
As we know, modulation index is given by,

μ=Amax-AminAmax+Amin

=14-614+6=0.4

subject: Physics
q: A signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math> is transmitted in a cable. The attenuation of cable is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>dB</mi></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and cable length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The power received at the receiver is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________. [Gain in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>dB</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>O</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math>]
question: A signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kW</mi></math> is transmitted in a cable. The attenuation of cable is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>dB</mi></math> per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and cable length is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The power received at the receiver is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _________. [Gain in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>dB</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><msub><mi>log</mi><mn>10</mn></msub><mfenced><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>O</mi></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mi>i</mi></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math>]
solution:
Sound level decreases by 5 dB every km so, sound level decreased in 20 km=100 dB

β2-β1=10 log10I2I1

-100=10 log10I2I1⇒I1I2=1010

I2=10-10I1⇒P2=10-10P1=10-8 W

x=8

subject: Physics
q: In the case of amplitude modulation to avoid distortion the modulation index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>μ</mi></mfenced></math> should be :
question: In the case of amplitude modulation to avoid distortion the modulation index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>μ</mi></mfenced></math> should be :
solution:
The modulation index of an amplitude modulated signal is defined as the measure or extent of amplitude variation about an un-modulated carrier.

Modulation index is  μ=AmAc

To avoid distortion, μ≤1 because  μ>1 will result in interference between career frequency and message frequency, resulting into distortion.

subject: Physics
q:
An antenna is placed in a dielectric medium of dielectric constant 6.25. If the maximum size of that antenna is 5.0 mm, it can radiate a signal of minimum frequency of _____ GHz.

 (Given μr=1 for dielectric medium)
question:
An antenna is placed in a dielectric medium of dielectric constant 6.25. If the maximum size of that antenna is 5.0 mm, it can radiate a signal of minimum frequency of _____ GHz.

 (Given μr=1 for dielectric medium)
solution:
We know that, refractive index is related to velocity as n=cv.

Here, n=εrμr=6.251=2.5

Since, effective length of antenna l>λ4⇒l>v4f

So, f>v4l=c4l2.5=3×1082.5×4×5×10-3=6×109 Hz=6 GHz.

subject: Physics
q: Amplitude modulated wave is represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>AM</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The total bandwidth of the amplitude modulated wave is
question: Amplitude modulated wave is represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mi>AM</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The total bandwidth of the amplitude modulated wave is
solution:
Expression for modulation is given by,

st=Ac1+μcos2πfmtcos2πfct, where fm and fc are the frequency of the modulating signal and the carrier signal respectively.

Comparing it with the given expression in question, we get,

fm=104 Hz

Now, Bandwidth =2fm=20 kHz

subject: Physics
q: By what percentage will the transmission range of a TV tower be affected when the height of the tower is increased by 21%?
question: By what percentage will the transmission range of a TV tower be affected when the height of the tower is increased by 21%?
solution:
The range of a TV tower antenna is given by 

d=2hR

New height is given as

h'=1.21h 

Let the new range be

 d'=2h'R⇒d'=1.212hR=1.1d

 So, the increase in the range is =1.1d-dd×100=10%

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I with List II

  List I   List II
A Attenuation I Combination of a receiver and transmitter
B Transducer II Process of retrieval of information from the carrier wave at receiver
C Demodulation III Converts one form of energy into another
D Repeater IV Loss of strength of a signal while propagating through a medium

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List I with List II

  List I   List II
A Attenuation I Combination of a receiver and transmitter
B Transducer II Process of retrieval of information from the carrier wave at receiver
C Demodulation III Converts one form of energy into another
D Repeater IV Loss of strength of a signal while propagating through a medium

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Attenuation is the loss of signal strength in networking cables or connections.

Transducers are used in electronic communications systems to convert signals of various physical forms to electronic signals, and vice versa.

Demodulation is defined as extracting the original information-carrying signal from a modulated carrier wave.

Repeaters are used to extend transmissions so that the signal can cover longer distances or be received on the other side of an obstruction.

subject: Physics
q: A carrier wave with amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is amplitude modulated by a sinusoidal base band signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of minimum amplitude to maximum amplitude for the amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A carrier wave with amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>250</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is amplitude modulated by a sinusoidal base band signal of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The ratio of minimum amplitude to maximum amplitude for the amplitude modulated wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
The value of maximum amplitude, Amax=Ac+Am=250+150 V=400 V
, and the value of minimum amplitude,Amin=Ac-Am=250-150 V=100 V
⇒AminAmax=100 V400 V=14=50200⇒x=200 V

subject: Physics
q:
An amplitude modulated signal is plotted below:
Which one of the following best describes the above signal?
question:
An amplitude modulated signal is plotted below:
Which one of the following best describes the above signal?
solution:
After analysing the graph we may conclude that

(i) Amplitude varies as $8-10 \mathrm{~V}$ or $9 \pm 1$

(ii) Two time period as

$100 \mu$ s (signal wave) $\& 8 \mu$ (carrier wave)

So, equation of AM signal is

$\left[9 \pm 1 \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi t}{T_{1}}\right) \sin \left(\frac{2 \pi t}{T_{2}}\right)\right]$

$=\left[9 \pm \sin \left(2 \pi \times 10^{4} \mathrm{t}\right)\right] \sin \left(2.5 \pi \times 10^{5} \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{V}$

subject: Physics
q: A bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> is available for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math> transmission. If the maximum audio signal frequency used for modulating the carrier wave is not to exceed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The number of stations that can be broadcasted within this band simultaneously without interfering with each other will be __________.
question: A bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> is available for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math> transmission. If the maximum audio signal frequency used for modulating the carrier wave is not to exceed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The number of stations that can be broadcasted within this band simultaneously without interfering with each other will be __________.
solution:
Band width =2×n× highest modulating frequency.
6MHz=2×n×6KHz
⇒ n=6×1062×6×103=500

subject: Physics
q: An amplitude modulated waves is represented by expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mo> </mo><mn>6280</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>211</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The minimum and maximum amplitudes of the amplitudes modulated wave are, respectively:
question: An amplitude modulated waves is represented by expression <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cos</mi><mo> </mo><mn>6280</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>211</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>. The minimum and maximum amplitudes of the amplitudes modulated wave are, respectively:
solution:
From Given Equation

μ=0.6

Am=μAc

Amax+Amin2=Ac=5 V

Amax-Amin2=3 V

From Equation (1) + (2)

Amax=8 V

From Equation (1) – (2)

Amin=2 V

subject: Physics
q: A telephonic communication service is working at a carrier frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>GHz</mi></math>. Only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of it is utilized for transmission. How many telephonic channels can be transmitted simultaneously if each channel requires a bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>?
question: A telephonic communication service is working at a carrier frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>GHz</mi></math>. Only <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of it is utilized for transmission. How many telephonic channels can be transmitted simultaneously if each channel requires a bandwidth of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>?
solution:
Number of telephonic channels that can be transmitted simultaneously, 

=0.1×10×1095×103=2×105

subject: Physics
q: If a carrier wave $\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}$ is amplitude modulated by a modulator signal $\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A}$ sin $\omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$ then the equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$ and its modulation index are respectively
question: If a carrier wave $\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}$ is amplitude modulated by a modulator signal $\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A}$ sin $\omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$ then the equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$ and its modulation index are respectively
solution:
Modulation index
$$
\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{c}}}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}=1
$$
Equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$
$$
=\mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{t}}
$$
$=\mathrm{A}\left(1+\sin \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{t}\right) \sin \omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$
$\left(\right.$ As $\left.\mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})}=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}\right)$

subject: Physics
q: A message signal of frequency <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> and peak voltage <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used execute amplitude modulation on a carrier wave of frequency <math><mn>300</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> and peak voltage <math><mi> </mi><mn>400</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> . The modulation index and difference between the two side band frequencies are:
question: A message signal of frequency <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> and peak voltage <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used execute amplitude modulation on a carrier wave of frequency <math><mn>300</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> and peak voltage <math><mi> </mi><mn>400</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> . The modulation index and difference between the two side band frequencies are:
solution:
Modulating index is given by,
μ=AmAe
=100 V400 V
=0.25
The difference between the maximum frequency of modulated wave and the minimum frequency of modulated wave is given by
Δf=fmax-fmin
=fc+fm-fc-fm
=2fm
=2(100 MHz)
=2×108 Hz

subject: Physics
q: A sinusoidal carrier voltage is amplitude modulated. The resultant amplitude modulated wave has maximum and minimum amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The amplitude of each side band is :
question: A sinusoidal carrier voltage is amplitude modulated. The resultant amplitude modulated wave has maximum and minimum amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The amplitude of each side band is :
solution:
Given here, 

Ac+Am=120    ...1

Ac-Am=80    ...2

From equation 1 and 2, we have

2Ac=200⇒Ac=100 V and Am=20 V

Modulation index μ=20100=15

Amplitude of each side band

=Acμ2

=100×110=10 volt

subject: Physics
q: The maximum and minimum voltage of an amplitude modulated signal are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The percentage modulation index will be
question: The maximum and minimum voltage of an amplitude modulated signal are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. The percentage modulation index will be
solution:
If amplitude of carrier wave is Ac and amplitude of message wave is Am,

Amax=Ac+Am and Amin=Ac-Am

Modulation index,

Mi=AmAc=Amax-Amin2Amax+Amin2=60-2060+20=12=50%

subject: Physics
q:
Given the electric field of a complete amplitude modulated wave as
$$
\vec{E}=\hat{i} E_c\left(1+\frac{E_m}{E_c} \cos \omega_m t\right) \cos \omega_c t
$$
Where the subscript c stands for the carrier wave and $\mathrm{m}$ for the modulating signal. The frequencies present in the modulated wave are
question:
Given the electric field of a complete amplitude modulated wave as
$$
\vec{E}=\hat{i} E_c\left(1+\frac{E_m}{E_c} \cos \omega_m t\right) \cos \omega_c t
$$
Where the subscript c stands for the carrier wave and $\mathrm{m}$ for the modulating signal. The frequencies present in the modulated wave are
solution:
The frequencies present in amplitude modulated wave are :
Carrier frequency $=\omega_c$
Upper side band frequency $=\omega_c+\omega_m$
Lower side band frequency $=\omega_c-\omega_m$.

subject: Physics
q: In satellite communication, the uplink frequency band used is:
question: In satellite communication, the uplink frequency band used is:
solution: In satellite telecommunication, a downlink is the link from a satellite down to one or more ground stations or receivers, and an uplink is the link from a ground station up to a satellite. Uplink frequency band for satellite communication is 5.925-6.425 GHz.

subject: Physics
q: Find the modulation index of an AM wave having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> variation where maximum amplitude of the AM wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
question: Find the modulation index of an AM wave having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> variation where maximum amplitude of the AM wave is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>.
solution:
Modulation index is given by m=AmAc, where, Am is amplitude of modulating signal and Ac is amplitude of carrier signal.

Given: 2Am=8

Am+Ac=9⇒Ac=5

∴m=45=0.8

subject: Physics
q: The modulation frequency of an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> radio station is <math><mn>250</mn><mi>k</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>,</mo></math> which is <math><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></math> of the carrier wave. If another <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> station approaches you for license what broadcast frequency will you allot?
question: The modulation frequency of an <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> radio station is <math><mn>250</mn><mi>k</mi><mi>H</mi><mi>z</mi><mo>,</mo></math> which is <math><mn>10</mn><mi>%</mi></math> of the carrier wave. If another <math><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> station approaches you for license what broadcast frequency will you allot?
solution:
Carrier wave frequency =250×10=2500 kHz

Frequency used 2500±250k Ω=2250 kHz to 2750 kHz

The allotted frequency showed be such that f±250 kHz should not overlap with previous one. Hence possible frequency =2000 kHz

subject: Physics
q: The TV transmission tower at a particular station has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. For doubling the coverage of its range, the height of the tower should be increased by
question: The TV transmission tower at a particular station has a height of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>125</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. For doubling the coverage of its range, the height of the tower should be increased by
solution:
Maximum distance covered is given by d=2Rh, here R is the radius.

Now, to increase the length of radio transmission to 2 times,2d=2h'R

Height of tower is h'=4h=4×125=500 m

\(\begin{aligned} \Delta H=h^{\prime}-h &=500-125 \\ &=375 \end{aligned}\)

subject: Physics
q: An amplitude modulated signal is given by $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{t})=10[1+0.3$ $\left.\cos \left(2.2 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{t}\right)\right] \sin \left(5.5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{t}\right) .$ Here $\mathrm{t}$ is in seconds. The sideband frequencies (in $\mathrm{kHz}$ ) are, [Given $\pi=22 / 7$ ]
question: An amplitude modulated signal is given by $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{t})=10[1+0.3$ $\left.\cos \left(2.2 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{t}\right)\right] \sin \left(5.5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{t}\right) .$ Here $\mathrm{t}$ is in seconds. The sideband frequencies (in $\mathrm{kHz}$ ) are, [Given $\pi=22 / 7$ ]
solution:
Equation given
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{t})=10\left[1+0.3 \cos \left(2.2 \times 10^{4}\right)\right] \\
\sin \left(5.5 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{t}\right) \\
=10+1.5\left[\sin \left(57.2 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{t}\right)+\sin \left(52.8 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{t}\right)\right] \\
\omega_{\mathrm{c}}+\omega_{\mathrm{w}}=57.2 \times 10^{4}=2 \pi \mathrm{f}_{1}
\end{array}
$$
$$
\mathrm{f}_{1}=\frac{57.2 \times 10^{4}}{2 \times\left(\frac{22}{7}\right)}=9.1 \times 10^{4} \simeq 91 \mathrm{KHz}
$$
$$
\omega_{\mathrm{c}}-\omega_{\mathrm{w}}=52.8 \times 10^{4}
$$
$$
\mathrm{f}_{2}=\frac{52.8 \times 10^{4}}{2 \times\left(\frac{22}{7}\right)} \simeq 84 \mathrm{KHz}
$$

Upper side band frequency $\left(f_{1}\right)$ is
$$
\mathrm{f}_{1}=\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{c}}-\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{w}}=\frac{52.8 \times 10^{4}}{2 \pi} \approx 85.00 \mathrm{kHz}
$$
Lower side band frequency $\left(\mathrm{f}_{2}\right)$ is
$$
\mathrm{f}_{2}=\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{c}}+\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{w}}=\frac{57.2 \times 10^{4}}{2 \pi} \approx 90.00 \mathrm{kHz}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A radio can tune to any station in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> band. The value of corresponding wavelength bandwidth will be
question: A radio can tune to any station in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> band. The value of corresponding wavelength bandwidth will be
solution:
Given: Frequency f1=6MHz

Frequency f2=10MHz

Wavelength can be calculated using λ=cf.

⇒λ1=cf1=3×1086×106=50 m

⇒λ2=cf2=3×10810×106=30 m

Wavelength bandwidth is the difference of upper lower wavelengths =λ1-λ2=20 m

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statement is NOT correct?
question: Which of the following statement is NOT correct?
options: Ground wave signals are more stable than the sky wave signals.
The critical frequency of an ionospheric layer is the highest frequency that will be reflected back by the layer when it is vertically incident.
Electromagnetic waves of frequencies higher than about $30 \mathrm{MHz}$ cannot penetrate the ionosphere.
Sky wave signals in the broadcast frequency range are stronger at night than in the day time.
solution: Above critical frequency $\left(f_c\right)$, an electromagnetic wave penetrates the ionosphere and is not reflected by it.

subject: Physics
q: A modulating signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>28</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>t</mi></math> is added to the carrier signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>t</mi></math> for amplitude modulation. The combined signal is passed through a non-linear square law device. The output is then passed through a band pass filter. The bandwidth of the output signal of band pass filter will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MHz</mi></math>.
question: A modulating signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>28</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>t</mi></math> is added to the carrier signal <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>t</mi></math> for amplitude modulation. The combined signal is passed through a non-linear square law device. The output is then passed through a band pass filter. The bandwidth of the output signal of band pass filter will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MHz</mi></math>.
solution:
Frequencies present in output of square law device

2fc,  fc+fm,  fc,  fc-fm,  2fm,  fm

After passing through band pass filter, frequencies present 

fc+fm,  fc,  fc-fm

Bandwidth of the output signal =2fm

=2ωm2π=6.28×1063.14 =2 MHz

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I with List II

  LIST I   LIST II
A AM Broadcast I 88-108 MHz
B FM Broadcast II 540-1600 kHz
C Television III 3.7-4.2 GHz
D Satellite Communication IV 54 MHz- 590 MHz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List I with List II

  LIST I   LIST II
A AM Broadcast I 88-108 MHz
B FM Broadcast II 540-1600 kHz
C Television III 3.7-4.2 GHz
D Satellite Communication IV 54 MHz- 590 MHz

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
AM broadcasting is radio broadcasting using amplitude modulation (AM) transmissions.

AM Broadcast range → 540–1600 kHz 

FM broadcasting is the method of radio broadcasting that uses frequency modulation (FM).

FM Broadcast range →88 – 108 MHz

Television →54 – 890 MHz

Satellite communication →3.7–4.2 GHz

∴A-II, B-I, C-IV, D-III

subject: Physics
q: An antenna is mounted on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> tall building. What will be the wavelength of signal that can be radiated effectively by the transmission tower upto a range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>44</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> ?
question: An antenna is mounted on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> tall building. What will be the wavelength of signal that can be radiated effectively by the transmission tower upto a range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>44</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> ?
solution:
d=2RhT

⇒44×103=2×6400×103×hT

⇒hT=151.25 m

∵ lantenna≥λ4

⇒λ≤4l

considering, hT=1= length of antenna

λ=4×151.25=605 m

subject: Physics
q: A message signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> is used to modulate a carrier signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math>. The bandwidth for amplitude modulation is:
question: A message signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> is used to modulate a carrier signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math>. The bandwidth for amplitude modulation is:
solution:
We have, 

Message frequency fm=5 kHz and carrier wave frequencyfc=2 MHz=2000 KHz

The resultant signal will have bandwidth of frequency given by

fc+fm-fc-fm

=[(2000+5)-(2000-5)] kHz

=10 kHz.

Thus, bandwidth for amplitude modulation is 10 kHz.
